content.text: What They Are and How the Format Evolved
) as distinct concepts. These have since been merged — files in either location create the same
Custom commands have been merged into skills. A file at
and work the same way. Your existing

files keep working. Skills add optional features: a directory for supporting files, frontmatter to control whether you or Claude invokes them, and the ability for Claude to load them automatically when relevant.
The core idea is simple: create a
file with instructions, and Claude adds it to its toolkit. Claude uses skills when relevant, or you can invoke one directly with

Claude Code skills follow the Agent Skills open standard, which works across multiple AI tools. Claude Code extends the standard with additional features like invocation control, subagent execution, and dynamic context injection.
In practical terms: if you keep pasting the same prompt into every session, it belongs in a custom command.
Every custom command is a directory containing a
file. The directory name determines the command name:

~/.claude/skills/my-command/
└── SKILL.md          # Creates /my-command (personal, all projects)

.claude/skills/my-command/
└── SKILL.md          # Creates /my-command (project-scoped, committed to git)

.claude/commands/
└── my-command.md     # Legacy single-file format, still supported
Where you store a skill determines who can use it. Enterprise-managed settings apply to all users in your organization. Personal skills at
are available across all your projects. Project skills at
apply to this project only. Plugin skills are scoped to wherever the plugin is enabled.

When skills share the same name across levels, enterprise overrides personal, and personal overrides project. A skill at any of these levels also overrides a bundled skill with the same name. For example, a
The skills directory also extends into monorepos. Project skills load from

in your starting directory and in every parent directory up to the repository root, so starting Claude in a subdirectory still picks up skills defined at the root. When you work with files in subdirectories below your starting directory, Claude Code also discovers skills from nested
If a nested skill shares a name with a root-level skill, both remain available. The nested one appears under a directory-qualified name,
, and its description says which directory it applies to.
The skill directory can contain more than just
. Each skill is a directory with

as the entrypoint. Other files are optional and let you build more powerful skills: templates for Claude to fill in, example outputs showing the expected format, scripts Claude can execute, or detailed reference documentation. Reference these files from your
so Claude knows what they contain and when to load them.

.claude/skills/code-review/
├── SKILL.md              # Main instructions (required)
├── templates/
│   └── review-report.md  # Template Claude fills in
├── examples/
│   └── good-review.md    # Positive example of expected output
└── scripts/
    └── gather-diff.sh    # Script Claude can run
file with two parts: YAML frontmatter between
markers that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with the instructions Claude follows when the skill runs. The directory name becomes the command you type, and the
helps Claude decide when to load the skill automatically.
A minimal skill looks like this:

---
description: Summarizes uncommitted changes and flags anything risky. Use when the user asks what changed, wants a commit message, or asks to review their diff.
---

## Current changes

!`git diff HEAD`

## Instructions

Summarize the changes above in two or three bullet points, then list any
risks you notice such as missing error handling, hardcoded values, or tests
that need updating. If the diff is empty, say there are no uncommitted changes.

git diff HEAD`` line uses dynamic context injection: Claude Code runs the command and replaces the line with its output before Claude sees the skill content, so the instructions arrive with the current diff already inlined.
The diagram below maps how a custom skill flows from disk to Claude's context when invoked:---
markers controls all aspects of how a skill is discovered, invoked, and executed. All fields are optional. Only
is recommended so Claude knows when to use the skill.
A maximally configured frontmatter looks like this:

---
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to production. Use when the user says deploy, ship, or release.
when_to_use: |
  - User says "deploy", "ship", "push to prod", or "release"
  - Do NOT use for staging; use /deploy-staging instead
argument-hint: [environment]
arguments: env branch
disable-model-invocation: true
user-invocable: true
allowed-tools: Bash Read Write
disallowed-tools: AskUserQuestion
model: claude-opus-4-6
effort: high
context: fork
agent: general-purpose
hooks:
  PreToolUse:
    - matcher: "Bash"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/validate-deploy.sh"
---

Deploy $env from branch $branch:
1. Run the full test suite
2. Build the application
3. Push to $env deployment target
4. Verify the deployment succeeded
The key fields and their effects:

is the most important field. It controls when Claude auto-invokes the skill, and must contain enough signal for Claude to match it against real user requests. A vague description like "helps with code" will never trigger. A specific description with concrete trigger terms works. Include the task type ("scan", "generate", "analyze"), the subject domain ("security", "API", "database"), and explicit trigger phrases. The combined
text is truncated at 1,536 characters in the skill listing to reduce context usage, so put the key use case first.
supplements the description. It provides additional context for when Claude should invoke the skill, such as trigger phrases or example requests. It is appended to
in the skill listing and counts toward the 1,536-character cap.
is purely a UX field: it shows a hint during autocomplete to indicate expected arguments, for example `
`. It does not affect argument processing.
declares named positional arguments. Named positional arguments allow `

attrs.latex: name` substitution in the skill content. Names map to argument positions in order. So `arguments: env branch` means `

content.text: maps to the first argument and
is essential for side-effect commands. Set to
to prevent Claude from automatically loading this skill. Use for workflows you want to trigger manually with
. Deployment commands, commits, and anything touching external systems should use this.
. Use for background knowledge users shouldn't invoke directly. Default:

pre-approves specific tools. Tools Claude can use without asking permission when this skill is active. This does not restrict available tools—it only removes the per-use approval prompt for the listed ones.
does the opposite: tools removed from Claude's available pool while this skill is active. Use for autonomous skills that should never call certain tools, such as
for a background loop. The restriction clears when you send your next message.
overrides the session model for the duration of the skill's turn. The override applies for the rest of the current turn and is not saved to settings; the session model resumes on your next prompt.
overrides the session effort level. Default: inherits from session. Options:
; available levels depend on the model.
runs the skill in an isolated subagent. Add

to your frontmatter when you want a skill to run in isolation. The skill content becomes the prompt that drives the subagent. It won't have access to your conversation history. This is useful for heavy research tasks, parallel processing, or workflows that would pollute the main context with verbose output.
limits auto-invocation by file path. When set, Claude loads the skill automatically only when working with files matching the patterns. Useful for per-package skills in a monorepo.
Custom commands accept arguments passed after the command name:
captures everything typed after the command name as a single string.
capture individual space-separated arguments by position. Named arguments declared in the
frontmatter field are referenced by name.
doesn't appear anywhere in the skill content, Claude Code auto-appends
to the end. This means even commands written without explicit placeholders will receive the arguments, though placement is less controlled.

---
argument-hint: <pr-number> <priority>
arguments: pr priority
---

Review PR #$pr with $priority priority.
Focus: security vulnerabilities, missing error handling, test coverage.
Reference our standards in [standards/code-review.md](standards/code-review.md).

attrs.latex: pr` maps to the first positional value and `

content.text: Environment variables from the shell are also available. The
variable, for instance, can be used in skill content to reference the current session identifier.
The most powerful capability of custom commands is the ability to run shell commands at invocation time and inject their output directly into the prompt Claude sees. The syntax is `

---
description: Summarize the current PR for review. Use when preparing a code review or asking "what does this PR do."
allowed-tools: Read Grep Glob
---

## PR context

- Diff: !`gh pr diff`
- Changed files: !`gh pr diff --name-only`
- PR description: !`gh pr view --json title,body -q '.title + "\n" + .body'`
- Recent commits: !`git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD`

## Your task

Summarize what this PR does, what decisions were made, and flag anything
that looks risky, incomplete, or missing tests. Structure as:
**Summary**, **Decisions**, **Risks**.

command`` syntax is a preprocessing directive in Claude Code skills and commands. It executes the shell command before the prompt is sent to Claude, replacing the directive with the command's output. Claude only ever sees the final text with the real data substituted in—it never sees the command itself.
Shell commands can be chained with pipes and standard shell operators:

## Critical paths changed

!`git diff --name-only HEAD~1 | grep -E '(auth|payment|security)' || echo "none"`

## Coverage gap check

!`npm test -- --coverage --passWithNoTests 2>&1 | tail -20`
command`` executes immediately when the skill loads. The output replaces the placeholder. Claude receives the fully-rendered prompt with actual data — not the commands themselves.

One important caution: shell injection is a footgun. The command runs on every invocation, and a failing command produces noisy output that the model may misinterpret. Use sparingly. Commands that may fail should include error fallbacks via
Skill files can contain any instructions, but thinking about how you want to invoke them helps guide what to include. Two patterns cover most use cases:
adds knowledge Claude applies to ongoing work. Conventions, patterns, style guides, domain knowledge. This runs inline alongside the existing conversation context:

---
name: api-conventions
description: API design patterns for this codebase. Load when writing or reviewing API endpoints.
---

When writing API endpoints in this codebase:
- Use RESTful naming with plural nouns: GET /users, POST /users, PATCH /users/:id
- Return `{ data, meta, errors }` envelope format
- Validate all inputs with Zod schemas before business logic
- Throw typed errors from `src/errors/` — never raw strings
- Include request logging via the `withRequestLog` middleware
gives Claude step-by-step instructions for a specific action. These are often actions you want to invoke directly with
rather than letting Claude decide when to run them. Add
to prevent Claude from triggering it automatically.

---
name: commit
description: Create a conventional commit from staged changes
disable-model-invocation: true
allowed-tools: Bash(git *)
argument-hint: [optional scope]
---

Create a conventional commit from the staged changes:

## Staged changes
!`git diff --cached --stat`
!`git diff --cached`

## Instructions
1. Analyze the diff above
2. Determine the type: feat / fix / chore / refactor / docs / test / perf / ci
3. Determine the scope from changed files: $ARGUMENTS (or infer from file paths)
4. Write a subject line under 72 characters, lowercase, no period
5. Run: `git commit -m "<type>(<scope>): <subject>"`
Skills can be invoked explicitly (you type
) and can also be auto-invoked by Claude when the command's description matches the task.
This dual-invocation model is controlled by frontmatter:

disable-model-invocation: true   → only manual /name invocation
user-invocable: false            → only auto-invocation by Claude
(neither set)                   → both manual and auto
The decision of which to use comes down to side effects. Anything that writes to a remote system, sends a message, deploys code, or runs irreversible operations should use
. Reference knowledge and analysis skills benefit from auto-invocation.

Auto-invoked skills are powerful but use them carefully. A skill that fires when you don't expect it is worse than no skill at all. Write precise descriptions that clearly delineate when a skill should and should not activate.
) provides a way to change invocation behavior for any skill without editing its frontmatter directly—useful for shared project skills or plugin-provided skills that cannot be modified:
command opens an interactive menu where these states can be cycled without editing JSON directly.
For workflows that would produce verbose output or need isolation from the main conversation,
runs the skill in a separate subagent context:

---
name: research-competitor
description: Deep research on a competitor product
disable-model-invocation: true
context: fork
agent: general-purpose
model: claude-opus-4-6
effort: high
---

Research the competitor product: $ARGUMENTS

1. Search for their latest product announcements and blog posts
2. Identify their primary feature differentiators
3. Note pricing model and target customers
4. Summarize in: Overview, Key Features, Pricing, Weaknesses, Our Opportunity

Write results to docs/research/$ARGUMENTS-analysis.md
The skill content becomes the prompt that drives the subagent. It won't have access to your conversation history.

only makes sense for skills with explicit instructions. If your skill contains guidelines like "use these API conventions" without a task, the subagent receives the guidelines but no actionable prompt, and returns without meaningful output.
Forked skills also have a separate tool permission scope—the
field in a forked skill grants those tools within the subagent's execution without requiring per-use approval from the main session.
Understanding the loading lifecycle helps explain why descriptions matter and where token costs arise.

Metadata (frontmatter fields) is always loaded at startup. Claude reads this first, for every available skill, in order to decide what might be relevant. This is why the description is so important for skills.
) get loaded when the skill is actually selected. This is where the workflow, steps, examples, and output expectations live.
Resources (supporting files such as documents, templates, schemas, or scripts placed in directories like
) are loaded only when needed during execution.

Once a skill loads, its content stays in context across turns, so every line is a recurring token cost. State what to do rather than narrating how or why, and apply the same conciseness test you would for

When many skills are present, description truncation can become an issue. If you have many skills, descriptions are shortened to fit the character budget, which can strip the keywords Claude needs to match your request. The budget scales at 1% of the model's context window. When it overflows, descriptions for the skills you invoke least are dropped first, so the skills you actually use keep their full text. Run
to see whether the budget is overflowing and which skills are affected.
environment variable to a fixed character count.

After a compaction event, Claude Code re-attaches the most recent invocation of each skill after the summary, keeping the first 5,000 tokens of each. Re-attached skills share a combined budget of 25,000 tokens. Skills used earlier in a long session may be dropped if many have been invoked. Re-invoking a skill after compaction restores its full content.
Claude Code watches skill directories for file changes. Adding, editing, or removing a skill under

directory takes effect within the current session without restarting. Creating a top-level skills directory that did not exist when the session started requires restarting Claude Code so the new directory can be watched.
This means iterating on a skill's instructions is a tight loop: edit the
, invoke the skill in the same session, observe the result, revise.

---
name: review-pr
description: Fetch and review a GitHub PR for bugs, security issues, and style. Use when the user provides a PR number or says "review PR."
argument-hint: <pr-number>
allowed-tools: Bash(gh *) Read Grep Glob
---

## PR context

- PR diff: !`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS`
- Comments so far: !`gh pr view $ARGUMENTS --comments`
- Changed files: !`gh pr diff $ARGUMENTS --name-only`
- CI status: !`gh pr checks $ARGUMENTS`

## Review checklist

Review the diff above for our team standards:
1. Logic errors and edge cases not covered by tests
2. Security: injection, auth bypass, secrets in code
3. Missing error handling or silent failures
4. Functions missing type annotations
5. Tests for new public functions

For each issue: cite the file and line number.
Severity: CRITICAL (blocks merge), WARNING (should fix), INFO (nice to have).

---
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to the specified environment
argument-hint: [staging|production]
disable-model-invocation: true
allowed-tools: Bash Read
effort: high
hooks:
  PreToolUse:
    - matcher: "Bash"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh"
---

Deploy to $ARGUMENTS:

## Pre-flight checks
!`git status --short`
!`git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD`

## Steps
1. Confirm all tests pass: run `npm test`
2. Build the production bundle: `npm run build`
3. Deploy to $ARGUMENTS: `npm run deploy:$ARGUMENTS`
4. Verify: `curl -f https://$ARGUMENTS.example.com/health`
5. Summarize what was deployed and confirm health check passed

---
name: db-conventions
description: Database and query conventions for this codebase. Load when writing SQL, modifying schema, or reviewing database code.
user-invocable: false
---

Database conventions for this project:
- All tables use snake_case naming
- Primary keys are `id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()`
- All timestamps: `created_at` and `updated_at` (managed by triggers)
- Soft deletes: `deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULL`
- Never use `SELECT *` in application code; list columns explicitly
- Migrations live in `db/migrations/`; always include a rollback
- Use parameterized queries only — never string concatenation

A description of "helps with code review" is useless for auto-invocation. Include the task type, the subject domain, and explicit trigger phrases like "when the user mentions" or "use when." Test by saying something you'd naturally say to trigger the command and seeing if Claude loads it.

Any command that deploys, commits, sends messages, or touches external systems should be manually invoked only. Claude auto-triggering a deploy command during a planning conversation is a painful mistake.
under 500 lines. Move detailed reference material to separate files in the same directory. Every line in an invoked skill is a token cost that recurs across every subsequent turn in the session.

only makes sense for skills with explicit instructions. If your skill contains guidelines like "use these API conventions" without a task, the subagent receives the guidelines but no actionable prompt, and returns without meaningful output.
. Arguments are space-separated — if an argument contains spaces, Claude receives it as multiple args.
Forgetting that skills share a name across levels take precedence in a specific order.

Enterprise > personal > project. If a personal skill with the same name as a project skill exists, the personal one wins, which can cause confusion when onboarding teammates who have different personal skills.
Dynamic injection commands that can fail silently.
fails with an error message if no PR number is available. This error text goes directly into Claude's context as the injected content, confusing the prompt. Add a fallback: !
gh pr diff 2>/dev/null || echo "No PR diff available — ensure gh is authenticated and run from a PR branch."
text is truncated at 1,536 characters in the skill listing. Content past that boundary is stripped before Claude sees it, which can eliminate the very trigger phrases that make auto-invocation work.
Match the skill format to the intended trigger.
for pure reference knowledge. Neither flag for analysis or summarization skills that benefit from being triggered both ways.
Write descriptions in the voice of a user request, not a technical spec.

"Summarizes uncommitted git changes. Use when the user asks what changed, wants a commit message, or asks to review their diff" is better than "Runs git diff and summarizes the output." The description is matched against user queries, so it should contain the phrases users actually type.
Commit project-level skills to version control.
are version-controlled and shared with your team — treat them like code, not documentation. Every developer who clones the repo gets the same slash commands. Document them in
so new contributors know they exist.
Even simple one-argument commands benefit from the autocomplete hint.
with no hint leaves teammates guessing.
Only pre-approve tools the skill genuinely needs. A security review skill that pre-approves
without a pattern gives blanket shell access without per-use confirmation.
After writing a skill, phrase several natural requests that should and should not trigger it. Run
to confirm the skill appears, then send the trigger phrases as messages to a fresh session and observe whether Claude loads the skill automatically. If it doesn't, sharpen the description.
(not tracked by git) for personal overrides to shared project skills. This lets one developer disable an auto-triggered skill they find noisy without affecting teammates.
Prefer project skills over personal skills for team workflows.
) are machine-local and not visible to teammates. Everything representing team standards, deployment procedures, or shared review checklists should be in

has two sections: YAML frontmatter that controls discovery and execution, and a markdown body with the instructions Claude follows when the skill runs.
is the most important frontmatter field—it controls auto-invocation and must use concrete trigger phrases that match what users actually type.
` executes a shell command at invocation time and replaces the placeholder with its output, grounding Claude's context in live project state.
,... (positional) substitute user-provided arguments into the skill body. Named arguments can be declared in the
is essential for any command that has side effects (deploy, commit, send) — it prevents Claude from triggering the command autonomously.
runs the skill in an isolated subagent with its own context window, keeping verbose research or long-running tasks from polluting the main conversation.
Once invoked, a skill's body stays in context for the rest of the session. Keep skill bodies concise and move reference material to supporting files in the skill directory.
